1: The book opens with divrey Yirmeyahu (diḇrê yirmĕyāhû, words of Jeremiah) — not the more common prophetic formula dĕḇar YHWH (word of YHWH). This is deliberate: Jeremiah is both the human speaker and the divine conduit; the book will contain both first-person prophetic oracles and biographical narrative. The prophet's lineage is given: ben-Hilqiyyahu min-hakohanim — a priestly family from Anathoth (modern Anata, northeast of Jerusalem). Being a priest-prophet (like Ezekiel) shapes Jeremiah's concern for cultic purity and covenant fidelity throughout the book.
2: The call-narrative uses a standard historical introduction: asher haya debar-YHWH elav (ʾăšer hāyâ dĕḇar-YHWH ʾēlāw) — the relative clause in the third person, marking the transition from book-title to historical setting. The thirteenth year of Josiah (ca. 627 BCE) places Jeremiah's call just after Josiah's accession but before the great reform of 621 BCE (2 Kings 22-23). Jeremiah's ministry thus preceded and likely informed the reform.
3: The superscription spans nearly half a century of ministry: Josiah (640-609), Jehoiakim (609-598), Zedekiah (598-586), ending with the Babylonian exile (galut Yerushalayim, gālût yĕrûšālayim). The phrase bĕḥōdeš haḥămîšî (in the fifth month) marks the destruction of the temple (586 BCE; 2 Kings 25:8-10). No other OT prophet's ministry is documented across so many successive crises.
4: The messenger formula wayhi davar-YHWH elay lemor (wayhî dĕḇar-YHWH ʾēlay lēʾmōr) — literally "and it came to pass, the word of YHWH to me, saying" — is the standard first-person prophetic reception formula. The word (dāḇār) arrives as an event with agency, not merely as an intellectual communication; the repeated use of this formula across the book signals each new oracle unit.
5: Beterem etsorcha babeten yadatikha (bĕṭerem ʾeṣōrkĕkā babbéṭen yĕdaʿtîkā): Before I formed you in the womb I knew you. Yādaʿtî is the Qal perfect of yādaʿ — the Bible's word for the most intimate knowing, including sexual union (Genesis 4:1, yādaʿ ʾet-ḥawwâ). This is not intellectual foreknowledge but covenantal election. Qiddaštîkā (qiddaštîkā) — Piel perfect of qādaš (to set apart as holy); the Piel intensifies: a comprehensive, decisive consecration. Nāḇîʾ laggôyim — prophet to the nations: the breadth of the commission is global from the start, even though Jeremiah will minister primarily in Jerusalem.
6: Jeremiah's protest follows the prophetic convention of inadequacy (hesitation topos): lo yādaʿtî dabbēr — I do not know how to speak; the verb yādaʿ (know) mirrors YHWH's election-knowing in v5 — Jeremiah claims not-knowing in precisely the area YHWH knows him for. Naʿar ʾānōkî — I am only a youth/child; naʿar can mean boy, servant, or young man (used of Joseph at 17, Genesis 37:2; of Solomon as king, 1 Kings 3:7). The objection is the same structure as Moses' speech-disability protest (Exodus 4:10) and Gideon's clan-weakness protest (Judges 6:15).
7: Al-tōmar naʿar ʾānōkî — Do not say, I am a youth. YHWH's refusal is grammatically a prohibition (jussive al + imperfect): the objection is closed. The counter-commission is twofold: el-kol-asher ešlāḥăkā telēk (to everyone I send you, you shall go) and veet kol-asher atzavvĕkā tĕdabbēr (everything I command you, you shall speak). The authority is transferred entirely to the sender: Jeremiah's adequacy is irrelevant because his words are YHWH's commands.
8: Al-tîrāʾ mippĕnêhem — do not be afraid of their faces. The construction mippĕnêhem (from their faces) is more visceral than our translation "of them" — it is the overwhelming human presence that produces fear. The antidote is kî-ittĕkā ʾănî lĕhaṣṣîlekā — for I am with you to deliver you; the divine presence (immanuel-pattern) grounds the command not to fear. This phrase forms an inclusio with v19 (same words closing the call-narrative).
9: Wayyišlaḥ YHWH ʾet-yādô — YHWH extended his hand. The reaching out of the divine hand is the investiture gesture of prophetic commission. Wayyagga‛ ʿal-pî — and touched my mouth: the mouth that claimed inadequacy (v6) is now supernaturally equipped. The mouth-touching parallels Isaiah 6:7 (the seraph's coal touches Isaiah's lips) and Ezekiel 3:1-3 (Ezekiel eating the scroll). Each commissioning act physically engages the prophetic speech-organ. Hinnēh nātattî dĕḇāray bĕpîkā — I have placed my words in your mouth: the prophet's words are now literally YHWH's words.
10: The commission is given as six infinitives: linttôš ûlintôts ûlĕhaʾăḇîd ûlahărôs ûliḇnôt ûlintôaʿ — to uproot, tear down, destroy, overthrow, build, and plant. The first four verbs are negative (deconstruction); the last two positive (reconstruction). This 4:2 ratio characterizes the entire book: the judgment oracles vastly outnumber the restoration promises. Yet the restoration verbs at the end are deliberate — Jeremiah's ministry, despite its overwhelming focus on judgment, has a salvific telos. The verbs bānâ (build) and nāṭaʿ (plant) recur in 31:28 as the marker of the new covenant era.
11: Maqēl šāqēd ʾănî rōʾeh — I see a branch of an almond tree. The vision is a visual pun: šāqēd is the almond tree (from the root šāqad, to watch/wake), known in Hebrew as "the waking tree" (hāʿēṣ hammĕšakkēm liphroaḥ) because it is the first tree to blossom after winter dormancy — the "watcher" that wakes in early spring. The visual image of the almond blossom sets up the wordplay in v12.
12: YHWH's interpretation: kî-šōqēd ʾănî ʿal-dĕḇārî laʿăśōtô — for I am watching over (šōqēd) my word to perform it. The pun is complete: šāqēd (almond, the watcher-tree) → šōqēd (watching). The almond's aggressive early blooming — its vigilant wakefulness at the first sign of spring — illustrates YHWH's equally vigilant, prompt performance of his prophetic word. The MKT cannot capture this wordplay; only the Hebrew makes the hermeneutic visible.
13: Sîr nāpûaḥ ûpānāyw mippĕnê ṣāpôn — a boiling/blown pot with its face (opening/mouth) from the north. The word nāpûaḥ (blown, from nāpaḥ) means the pot is actively being blown into flame from underneath; it is at full boil. Pānāyw (its face/opening) is the direction from which it tilts — the scalding liquid will pour southward toward Jerusalem when the pot tips from the north.
14: Mitstsāpôn tippātaḥ hārāʿâ — disaster will break open/crack from the north. The verb pātaḥ (break open) is unusual in this context; most translations say "break out" but the Hebrew more precisely describes a sealed vessel cracking open — the disaster has been sealed/restrained and now erupts. The "north" (ṣāpôn) is a theological rather than purely geographic direction in Jeremiah; Babylon lies due east, but invading armies would approach Jerusalem along the north-south road through Syria.
15: The enemies are kol-mišpeḥôt mamlekhôt ṣāpôn — all the families/clans of the kingdoms of the north. The collective noun mišpeḥôt (families, clans) applied to kingdoms is notable — it personalizes the political entities as kinship units. The image of each setting his throne at Jerusalem's gates (natanû ʾîš kissĕʾô petaḥ šaʿărê Yĕrûšālāyim) depicts a siege assembly with administrative occupation — the gates are where kings sit in judgment.
16: Triple charges of covenant violation: ʿāzāḇûnî (they abandoned me) — the verb ʿāzaḇ for covenant desertion is common in Deuteronomic texts; wayĕqattĕrû lēʾlōhîm ʾăḥērîm (burned incense to other gods) — the Piel of qāṭar denotes the specific act of incense-burning, a priestly act misdirected to idols; wayyištaḥăwû lĕmaʿăśēh yĕdêhem (bowed down to the work of their hands) — the Hishtaphel of ḥāwâ is the strongest prostration term, used for the most complete act of submission.
17: ʾĀzōr ḥalṣêkā wĕqamtā — gird your loins and arise. The idiom "gird the loins" (ʾāzar ḥalāṣāyim) means to tuck up the lower garment for action — running, fighting, working. It is the preparation posture of the Passover night (Exodus 12:11) and Elijah's running (1 Kings 18:46). The double command (gird and arise) signals decisive movement from the call to the commission. Pĕn-ʾăḥittĕkā lĕpānêhem — lest I terrify you before them: the threat cuts both ways — fear of the people produces fear from YHWH.
18: Three structural metaphors for the prophet's invulnerability: ʿîr miḇṣār (fortified city), lĕʿammûd barzel (iron pillar), lĕḥômôt nĕḥōšet (bronze walls). Each metaphor builds on the previous: a fortified city withstands siege; an iron pillar is structurally indestructible; bronze walls are impenetrable. The opponents listed — kings, officials, priests, and the people — constitute the entire social hierarchy; Jeremiah will face all of them.
19: Wĕnilḥămû ʾēlêkā wĕlōʾ-yûklû lāk — they will fight against you but will not overcome you. The verb lākad (overcome/prevail against) in the Qal indicates total defeat; the negation is absolute. The closing promise kî-ittĕkā ʾănî lĕhaṣṣîlekā repeats v8's inclusio verbatim — the call-narrative ends where it began: the divine presence as the sole guarantee of the prophet's mission.
